headlines.geopolitics: Navy veteran with no family gets full military honors as hundreds refuse to let him be buried alone
headlines.economy: DOJ creates new fraud division to combat 'insane' problem of billions stolen from government programs
headlines.domestic: Americans expect AI to transform everything but say it's mostly useless in their daily lives right now

metadata.daily_do: Check if your local VA cemetery has unclaimed veteran services you can support

clips.caption: When Pringle explains why we honor unknown soldiers
Duke's take on government fraud will surprise you
The AI prediction that aged like milk

script.text: The Tomb of the Unknown Soldier exists because we decided that dying alone in service to your country is the one thing we will never allow.
Good morning, I'm Leo Barros and this is The Friction. It's Monday, March 10th, 2026.

Let me get you caught up. First, geopolitics — hundreds of people showed up in Tennessee last week to give full military honors to Navy veteran Lonnie Wayman, who had no known family to claim his remains.
Oh wow.
The VA called him 'unclaimed' but the community said that was wrong — they claimed him. Full ceremony, doves released, the works.
That's how it should be.
Economy — Deputy AG Todd Blanche announced a new DOJ fraud division after what he calls an 'insane' amount of taxpayer money being stolen from government programs.
He cited cases like two guys from Philadelphia who got four million in federal funds to run homeless shelters in Minneapolis that didn't exist.
Four million? Jesus.
How does that even happen?
And domestic — a new Fox News poll shows eighty-seven percent of Americans expect AI to dramatically change our lives, but only forty-seven percent find it useful right now.
So we're all waiting for the robot apocalypse but can't get ChatGPT to write a decent grocery list.
Sounds about right.
Also, seventy-four percent of Americans believe life exists on other planets, which feels like the most normal thing in this poll.
Well, the universe is rather large.
Let's start with Lonnie Wayman. This story hit different than most military news because it's not about policy or budgets — it's about how we honor people.
Bree, you've looked at veteran services from the policy side. What does this tell us about the system?
I mean, first off, this community did something beautiful. But the fact that we have 'unclaimed' veterans at all points to a bigger breakdown.
The VA tracks veterans through their benefits, but if someone doesn't use services, or if their family situation is complicated, they can just... disappear from the system.
But hold on — this isn't a government failure. This is government working exactly as it should.
The VA stepped up, the community stepped up, full honors were given. What more do you want the federal government to do, track down every distant cousin?
I'm not saying track down cousins, but maybe we could do better outreach while veterans are alive? Social workers, community connections, something so people don't die alone in the first place?
Can I just say something? The fact that hundreds of strangers showed up for this guy is the most American thing I've heard in months.
Exactly. That's not the government solving a problem, that's people solving a problem.
But Duke, why does it have to be either-or? Like, yes, this community response was incredible, but we could also have better systems.
There are probably veterans dying alone every week who don't get this kind of attention because their story doesn't make the news.
That's actually a fair point. How many Lonnie Waymans are there that we don't hear about?
Look, I served. I know guys who came back and just wanted to be left alone. That's their choice. You can't save everyone from themselves.
I'm not talking about forcing anyone. I'm talking about making sure the option for connection exists.
This is gonna sound weird, but I looked up this guy's obituary. Lonnie Wayman. Served in the Navy, lived in Nashville, liked fishing.
That's it. That's all we know about him. And somehow hundreds of people showed up because they decided that was enough.
If I may interject briefly — this connects to something much older.
Professor, you mentioned the Tomb of the Unknown Soldier in your cold open. Take us there.
Yeah, I want to hear this.
Actually, let's give the Professor his full segment for this. This feels important.
The story of Lonnie Wayman connects to one of humanity's oldest anxieties — the fear of dying forgotten.
After World War One, every nation struggled with the same problem: thousands of unidentified dead. Bodies blown apart, dog tags lost, entire units obliterated.
The British started it in Westminster Abbey, November 1920. They selected one unidentified soldier from the Western Front and declared him representative of all the unknown dead.
That's beautiful actually.
America followed suit. Arlington National Cemetery, November 11th, 1921. The ceremony was extraordinary — President Harding, foreign dignitaries, thousands of mourners.
But here's what matters: they didn't just bury a body. They created a ritual that says 'no American who serves dies without honor, even if we don't know their name.'
That's exactly what happened in Tennessee.
Precisely. The community looked at Lonnie Wayman and said 'we may not know your family, but we know what you gave us.' That's the same impulse.
The Romans had a phrase: 'Dulce et decorum est pro patria mori' — it is sweet and fitting to die for one's country.
Wait, is that the poem? 'Dulce et decorum est'?
Wilfred Owen used that line, yes. He called it 'the old lie' after witnessing the horror of gas attacks in the trenches.
But here's what Owen missed — it's not about the dying being sweet. It's about the living refusing to let the dying be meaningless.
Oh wow, that's... that reframes everything.
The Tomb of the Unknown Soldier isn't about glorifying death. It's about communities declaring that service creates a debt we pay with memory.
So when the VA representative said Lonnie wasn't 'unclaimed' because the community claimed him...
He was invoking a principle that goes back millennia. The living have an obligation to honor the dead who served. It's one of the oldest social contracts we have.
And that's why it doesn't need to be a government program. It's deeper than that.
I mean, I still think we could do better outreach, but... I get it. This isn't about bureaucracy.
Doc, that was beautiful. I'm gonna think about that for a while.
The best honor we can give Lonnie Wayman is to remember that his story isn't unique. There are others like him, and the community response matters every single time.
Alright, let's shift gears to something that'll make us all less emotional — government fraud.
Ha! Speak for yourself.
Deputy AG Todd Blanche says the fraud problem is 'insane' and they're creating an entire new division to deal with it.
Two guys from Philadelphia got four million dollars to run homeless shelters in Minneapolis that didn't exist. Daycare centers that don't exist getting millions for school lunches.
Okay, this actually makes me furious. Like, genuinely furious.
Good! You should be furious. This is your tax money getting stolen.
No, but Duke, I'm furious for a different reason. These programs exist to help people who actually need help.
When people steal from homeless services or school lunch programs, they're literally taking food out of kids' mouths.
And that's exactly why these programs don't work. Too much money, not enough oversight.
No, Duke, that's not— the programs work when they're not being stolen from. The problem isn't the programs, it's the thieves.
But if the system makes it easy to steal, then the system is the problem. Blanche said they give money away 'like candy' with no checks.
Can I just say, as someone who's been broke — four million dollars for fake homeless shelters is some next-level sociopathy.
The question is whether a new DOJ division is the right approach. Bree, you deal with federal bureaucracy — does this make sense?
I mean, if the fraud is as widespread as Blanche says, then yeah, you need dedicated resources. But the real fix is better upfront verification.
Or just smaller programs that are easier to monitor. You can't steal four million dollars if there's only two million in the pot.
But then you help fewer people! That's not a solution, that's just shrinking the problem.
If half the money's getting stolen anyway, you're not helping as many people as you think you are.
Alright, hold on. Do we actually know how much is being stolen? Blanche gave examples, but what's the scale here?
That's actually a great question. Like, is this a billion-dollar problem or a hundred-billion-dollar problem? Because the response should be proportional.
Does it matter? Blanche said no fraud is too small. If you steal a thousand and one dollars, you go to prison. I like that.
A thousand and one? That's oddly specific. Is a thousand dollars the misdemeanor threshold?
In most states, yes. A thousand and one makes it a felony.
So if you steal taxpayer money, you get the full treatment. Good. Should be a deterrent.
I agree with the enforcement, I just don't want this to become an excuse to gut social programs.
Fair enough. We'll see how this plays out. Let's move to our AI story, which is somehow both more and less futuristic than expected.
Alright, rapid fire time. First up — eighty-seven percent of Americans think AI will change everything, but only forty-seven percent find it useful right now.
So basically we're all convinced the robots are coming but we can't get Siri to understand our accent.
I asked ChatGPT to help me fix my lawnmower last week. It told me to check if it was plugged in. It's a gas mower.
Oh no, that's actually hilarious.
But seriously, eighty percent of people are worried AI is eroding trust in news and social media. Which is fair, considering I saw a deepfake of my own mother last week.
Wait, what?
Someone used AI to make a video of her endorsing crypto. She doesn't even know what crypto is. She calls it 'computer money.'
That's deeply disturbing.
The weird part is the poll says sixty percent of people think they can tell if something is AI-generated. I guarantee you my mom would have believed that video.
That confidence is terrifying. Like, we're all walking around thinking we're AI detectives when the technology is getting better every day.
Jenny showed me a TikTok last night that looked completely real. Turns out it was AI. I couldn't tell at all.
Speaking of things that sound fake but aren't — Palantir's AI is apparently being used to find chairs for government employees.
I'm sorry, what?
Yeah, we've got military-grade AI surveillance technology optimized for... office furniture logistics.
That's the most government thing I've ever heard.
At least they're not using it to spy on us. Just... finding chairs.
Next story — a boss in Ireland repeatedly shouted 'potato' at his Irish employee and it's now a workplace harassment case.
Good heavens.
Like, imagine going to HR and saying 'my boss keeps yelling vegetables at me.'
That's not funny, Jax. That's genuinely awful harassment based on stereotypes.
No, you're right. It's completely messed up. I just can't wrap my head around the mindset of someone who thinks that's okay.
Moving on — Louisiana classrooms are required to post the Ten Commandments by Friday.
That's gonna be a lawsuit.
Yeah, that's pretty clearly unconstitutional. Establishment clause violation.
And finally, a Florida drug dealer locked a man in a storage unit for months over unpaid debt, which is probably the most Florida crime I've heard this year.
Jesus. Is the guy okay?
He was rescued, thankfully. But imagine explaining that to your family. 'Hey, I'm gonna be late for dinner, I'm locked in a storage unit.'
On that cheerful note, let's wrap up.
For today's Daily Do — the Lonnie Wayman story got me thinking. Check if your local VA cemetery has services for unclaimed veterans.
Many have volunteer programs where community members can attend these ceremonies. It's a small thing, but it matters.
That's really beautiful, Leo.
I'm gonna look that up when I get home.
As Winston Churchill said, 'A nation that forgets its defenders will be itself forgotten.'
Thanks for hanging with us today. I'm Jax Mooney.
And I'm Leo Barros. We'll see you tomorrow.
